summary: The main claim is: I think that developers implementing responsible scaling policies now increases the probability of effective regulation.
I think the risk from rapid AI development is very large, and that even very good RSPs would not completely eliminate that risk.

top_comment.author: Roko
top_comment.text: This really needs to be shouted from the rooftops. In the public sphere, people will hear "responsible scaling policy" as "It's maximally safe to keep pushing ahead with AI" rather than "We are taking on huge risks because politicians can't be bothered to coordinate".
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: In the public sphere, people will hear "responsible scaling policy" as "It's maximally safe to keep pushing ahead with AI" rather than "We are taking on huge risks because politicians can't be bothered to coordinate".

summary: The main claim is: I think the crispest explanation of how to update one's own maladaptive beliefs/expectations/parts/traumas, especially when beliefs get stuck and the usual methods don't work.
The main claim is: In principle, the squiggle maximizer could increase its intelligence and make squiggles in parallel.

This means that the future of earth under the condition that an uncontrollable AI is developed seems quite foreseeable (even though of course no one knows what kind of technology a superintelligent AI would use to increase its intelligence…
The main claim is: Joint: the model is trained end-to-end using a loss combined of the concept prediction error and class prediction error.
These models, when trained end-to-end, naturally put additional relevant information into the concept layer, which is encoded from run to run in very similar ways .
The main claim is: The Pink Shoggoth doesn't ask for much in return, at least nothing humans can give to it.
The main claim is: END is the last token, the one right before IO should be predicted We call the prompt an ABBA prompt if IO occurs before S1, and a BABA prompt if S1 occurs before IO.

The main claim is: Based on interviews with seven SERI MATS mentors, the qualities most valued in junior alignment researchers are perseverance through directionless periods, resourcefulness and social agency when stuck, and strong concrete models of AI safety.
Strong inside-view models of safety are prized but slow to acquire, usually only coming from extended time spent solving problems and talking with people who already have good models.

top_comment.author: Leon Lang
top_comment.text:
There may also be social reasons to give different answers depending on whether you are a mentor or mentee. I.e., answering "the better mentees were those who were smarter" seems like an uncomfortable thing to say, even if it's true. 

(I do not want to say that this social explanation is the only reason that answers between mentors and mentees differed. But I do think that one should take it into account in one's models)
top_comment.claim: The comment's point: There may also be social reasons to give different answers depending on whether you are a mentor or mentee.

summary: The main claim is: I think there’s exciting work to be done, both on using toy models to find general principles of how neural networks learn and on understanding the training dynamics in real language models.
- I would start by training a 2L attn only model on repeated random tokens to form induction heads.
The main claim is: Deceptive alignment is either a necessary component of, or substantially worsens, nearly every catastrophic AI scenario, so it deserves central attention in AGI safety.
Most failure modes look much worse once deception is added, which is why senior researchers often implicitly mean deceptive alignment when they discuss misalignment.
The main claim is: This suggests that we should do reward modeling , where we learn a model of the reward function from human feedback.
To this end, we would like to train our reward model on hypothetical trajectories.
